concepts.title: Equation Of Locus
concepts.conceptBody:
Locus

Definition: When a point moves so as always to satisfy a given condition, or conditions, the path it traces out is called its Locus under these conditions.

For example, suppose $\text{O}$ to be a given point in the plane of the paper and that a point $\text{P}$ is to move on the paper so that its distance from $\text{O}$ shall be constant and equal to a. It is clear that all the positions of the moving point must lie on the circumference of this circle whose centre is $\text{O}$ and whose radius is a. The circumference of this circle is therefore the "Locus" of $\text{P}$ when it moves subject to the condition that its distance from $O$ shall be equal to the constant distance $\alpha $.

Again, suppose A and B to be two fixed points and that the point $\text{P}$ is to move in the plane of the paper so that the angle APB is always a right angle. If we describe a circle on $\text{AB}$ as diameter then $\text{P}$ may be any point on the circumference of this circle, since the angle in a semi-circle is a right angle; also it could easily be shown that APB is not a right angle except when $\text{P}$ lies on this circumference. The "Locus" of $\text{P}$ under the assumed condition is therefore a circle on $\text{AB}$ as diameter.

Example - If a point $P$ moves in a plane such that whose distance from two fixed points $A$ and $B$ (say) are always equal i.e., $\mathbf{PA}=\mathbf{PB}$. 

(The point $\text{P}$ cannot be at $\text{Q}$ because $\text{AQ}\ne \text{BQ}$ )

Obviously, all the positions of the moving point $\text{P}$ lies on the right bisector of $\text{AB}$. Thus the locus of the moving point $\text{P}$ is the right bisector of $\text{AB}.$

Equation of a Locus 

A relation $\text{f}\left( \text{x},\text{y} \right)=0$ between $\text{x}$ and $\text{y}$ which is satisfied by each point on the locus and such that each point satisfying the equation is on the locus is called the equation of the locus.

How to find the Locus of a Point 

  1. Let $\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}},{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right)$ be the co-ordinates of the moving point say $\text{P}$.
  2. Now apply the geometrical conditions on ${{x}_{1}},{{y}_{1}}$. This gives a relation between ${{x}_{1}}$ and ${{y}_{1}}$.
  3. Now replace ${{\text{x}}_{1}}$ by $\text{x}$ and ${{\text{y}}_{1}}$ by $\text{y}$ in the eliminant and resulting equation would be the equation of the locus.

Note: 

  1. To find the locus of the point of intersection of two straight lines, eliminate the parameter or parameters from the given lines. If more than one parameter, then additional condition or conditions will also be given.
  2. Simplify the equation by squaring both sides if square roots are there and taking LCM to remove the denominators.

Q) A point moves so that the sum of the squares of its distances from two fixed points A $\left( \text{a},0 \right)$ and $\text{B}\left( -\text{a},0 \right)$ is constant and equal to $2{{\text{c}}^{2}}$, find the locus of the point. 

Solution: 

Let $\text{P}\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}},{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right)$ be the moving point whose locus is required.

By hypothesis

$(\mathrm{PA})^2+(\mathrm{PB})^2=2 \mathrm{c}^2$

$\Rightarrow$ $\left(\mathrm{x}_1-\mathrm{a}\right)^2+\left(\mathrm{y}_1-0\right)^2+\left(\mathrm{x}_1+\mathrm{a}\right)^2+\left(\mathrm{y}_1-0\right)^2=2 \mathrm{c}^2$

$\Rightarrow$ $2 \mathrm{x}_1^2+2 \mathrm{y}_1^2+2 \mathrm{a}^2=2 \mathrm{c}^2$

or $\mathrm{x}_1^2+\mathrm{y}_1^2=\mathrm{c}^2-\mathrm{a}^2$

Changing $\left(\mathrm{x}_1, \mathrm{y}_1\right)$ to $(\mathrm{x}, \mathrm{y})$ then

$x^2+y^2=c^2-a^2$

which is required locus of P.
